title: Paper clay wearable brooch
subtitle: A small sculpted brooch in paper clay with a pin back
excerpt: A small sculptural brooch formed from paper clay, carved and detailed before drying, then finished with acrylic paint and a metal pin back. Paper clay's cellulose fibres make it strong at the fine-detail scale needed for jewellery. No kiln required.
sourceNotes: Paper-clay-based wearables have developed alongside the craft jewellery movement. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A clay body with cellulose fibres added, which knit the structure together for added strength — especially useful at small scales and thin sections.
Fully air-dried. For paper clay, the structural state before finishing.

content.text: Paper clay is the right choice for wearable jewellery because the cellulose fibres hold thin sections together through the drying and finishing process. Plain air-dry clay chips and cracks at the fine edges needed for a brooch. This tutorial makes one brooch: a small sculptural form about 40 mm across. The shape can be botanical (a leaf cluster, a sprig), geometric (a rounded rectangle, a circle), or freely sculpted.

items.name: Strong adhesive (two-part epoxy or strong PVA)

content.text: Steps
Form 30 g of paper clay into an oval roughly 40 mm across and 6 mm thick.
Carve texture and detail into the surface with a needle tool.
Trim the outer edge clean with a fettling knife and round it slightly.
Dry flat to bone-dry over 24 hours, then sand smooth and seal with varnish.
Glue a brooch back to the reverse with strong adhesive.
Forming the base shape
Take 30 g of
paper clay

and press it into a rough oval or free shape about 40 mm across and 6 mm thick on the board. Work from the centre outward, pressing with the heel of the hand. The surface does not need to be perfectly flat; slight undulation is part of the handmade character.
Adding detail

Carve texture and detail into the surface with the needle tool. For a botanical leaf cluster: press thin leaf shapes cut from a 3 mm slab onto the base shape with a little slurry, then score veins with the needle tool. For a geometric form: incise lines and depressions 1-2 mm deep. For a free sculptural shape: build up small coils of clay on the surface to create relief, smoothing each addition with the rib.
Refining the edges

Trim the outer edge with the fettling knife to clean up the silhouette. Round the edge slightly with a finger to prevent chipping. Smooth the back face flat with the rib so the brooch lies against fabric without digging in.
Drying and finishing
Lay the brooch face-up on the board to dry to
bone-dry

over 24 hours. Sand any rough spots with 220-grit sandpaper, working carefully around the detail. Paint with acrylics, then seal with two coats of PVA or acrylic varnish. Once fully dry, glue the pin back to the smooth reverse face using two-part epoxy or strong contact adhesive. Allow the adhesive to cure fully before wearing.
Care

Paper clay brooches are not waterproof. Do not wear in rain or wash with water. The varnish protects against everyday humidity but not direct wetting. Attach only to firm-weave fabrics; fine fabrics may distort under the pin weight for pieces larger than 35 mm across.
Variations
Make a matched set of three small brooches: a mushroom, a leaf, and a small bird, all in the same 25-30 mm size.
Build the piece on a thin slab base to get clean edges. Cut the base to shape with the fettling knife before adding surface detail.
Replace the pin back with a clip fitting for a hair clip. Size up to 50 mm across and increase thickness to 8 mm for a hair-clip scale.

items.cause: Detail was added when clay was too dry, or plain air-dry clay was used instead of paper clay
items.fix: Add detail only to fresh, soft clay. Use paper clay throughout; the cellulose fibres hold fine edges and joins

items.symptom: Pin back detaches from the reverse
items.cause: Surface was insufficiently sanded before gluing, or adhesive did not cure
items.fix: Sand the back with 220 grit, wipe clean, and use two-part epoxy with full cure time before wearing
Place the brooch under a light weight (a smooth tile) for the first hour of drying to keep it flat
